topic: A college student discovers their roommate has been reading their journal
raw_output:
[story 1]

The air in the room hung thick with the smell of stale coffee and old textbooks. I was slumped over my desk, tracing patterns on the worn cover of a philosophy text, my gaze drifting toward the corner where Liam usually sprawled. Today, he was still, perched on the edge of his bed, a book open in his lap. It was my journal. A cold knot tightened in my stomach, a slow, heavy pressure building behind my ribs. I didn't move, just watched his eyes flick down, then back up, a brief, unreadable exchange. When he closed the book with a soft snap, the silence that followed felt enormous. I picked up my pen, the plastic feeling alien in my hand. I didn't confront him. I just started writing again, the scratch of the nib against the paper the only sound in the room.

[story 2]

It was Tuesday afternoon, the kind of grey light that seeped through the dusty windowpanes of our shared room. I was attempting to zone out, scrolling aimlessly through my phone, the usual low hum of apathy settling over me like a heavy blanket. Then I noticed the journal lying open on my nightstand. My breath hitched, a shallow, involuntary intake of air. I looked over at Liam, who was ostensibly studying, his posture rigid. He didn't look up immediately. When he finally did, his expression was carefully neutral, a practiced blankness that made my skin prickle. "Everything alright?" he asked, his voice flat, devoid of any real concern. I just shrugged, a small, dismissive movement. "Yeah. Just... reading." The word hung there, heavy with unspoken implication.

[story 3]

I found it tucked beneath a pile of laundry—my journal. The realization hit me not with a jolt, but with a dull, pervasive sense of déjà vu. I picked it up, the paper slightly warped where Liam had been leaning over it. My fingers brushed against a page filled with my most private anxieties, transcribed in my own handwriting. I didn't feel anger, or even surprise. Just a vast emptiness spreading through my chest, like a slow leak. I placed the journal back down, deliberately not making eye contact with Liam, who was engrossed in a video game across the room. I simply started organizing my scattered notes, the mundane task a shield against the sudden, unwelcome awareness of being observed.
